narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 and LSAT score of 29, both below the typical averages of 3.21 and 36.70 seen among past students. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 79.3% chance that you would fail it— a high-risk threshold that led to the rejection of your application. This prediction wasn't random; it was driven primarily by your academic performance in school, specifically your low rankings in both your first-year and third-year grade deciles, where you placed in the bottom decile (1 out of 10) compared to averages around the middle of the pack. Your third-year decile rank had the strongest pull toward this failure prediction, as it signaled to the model a consistent struggle with coursework that often foreshadows bar exam challenges for students in similar positions. Your first-year decile rank reinforced this concern, pushing the prediction further in the same direction by highlighting early academic hurdles that many successful bar passers overcome. On a more positive note, your upper-middle family income provided a slight counterbalance, nudging the model a bit toward expecting success, likely because students from similar backgrounds sometimes access extra resources that help. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your combination of low decile ranks sets you apart as riskier, even with your full-time commitment, which is the norm for over 90% of applicants. While factors like your LSAT and GPA played roles, it was these decile rankings that dominated the decision. This doesn't define your potential, especially as a determined Black man navigating these paths where systemic barriers can impact early metrics. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average, strengthening your undergrad record through post-bac courses or a master's program, and seeking targeted bar prep resources tailored for underrepresented students. Many in your position have succeeded by addressing these gaps head-on—reach out to admissions counselors for feedback and reapply after building a stronger academic story. Your full-time dedication is a great foundation; channeling it into these improvements could shift the odds dramatically next time.
